edge: There is a well-understood relationship between model complexity and prediction error, typically presented as a U-shaped curve reflecting a transition between under- and overfitting regimes.

Recent influential work has suggested that conventional statistical wisdom regarding the relationship between model complexity and prediction error may be incomplete, introducing an additional regime that exhibits a second descent in test error as the parameter count grows past sample size - a phenomenon dubbed double descent, particularly observed in overparametrized neural networks.

While most attention has been given to the deep-learning setting, double descent phenomena have been shown to emerge more generally across non-neural models such as linear regression, trees, and boosting.

In this work, the authors take a closer look at the evidence surrounding classical statistical machine learning methods and challenge the claim that observed cases of double descent truly extend the limits of a traditional U-shaped complexity-generalization curve therein.
The second descent in test error appears as the parameter count grows past sample size n.

There are implicitly multiple, distinct complexity axes along which the parameter count grows, leading to the appearance of a second descent in test error when the transition between these underlying axes occurs.
The location mentioned is not inherently tied to the interpolation threshold.
The investigated methods are interpreted as smoothers from a classical nonparametric statistics perspective.

node_2: alternative path to understanding double descent in deep learning
edge: The approach pursued in this work could potentially provide an alternative path to understanding double descent in the case of deep learning, which is arguably its most prominent setting.
It may be instructive to investigate whether there also exist multiple implicitly entangled complexity axes in neural networks, which could help to explain double descent in that setting.

One promising approach to bridging this gap could be to combine insights of Sec. 3 with the known connections between random feature models and two-layer neural networks, and stochastic gradient descent and min-norm solutions.

Combining insights from Sec. 3 with known connections between random feature models and two-layer neural networks, and stochastic gradient descent and min-norm solutions is considered a fruitful and non-trivial direction for future research.
